speaker: interviewer
text: Go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, so the-- after three weeks, nobody knows what's going to happen. I found out about it only at night that-- that they built the gallows there. I was still working for the [? lagers. ?] And instead of-- they march everybody to the-- as usual, when the commanders came in, with the music, just like in Auschwitz. It was regularly there to march to-- to-- to-- to-- to the music, although the could hardly walk, and they were counted. They had to match on the-- on the parade ground, and each block was counted, and [? blockalteste ?] reported how many people there to make sure that nobody is missing. And then each of-- the minute the report was over, everyone was happy, can go to-- to the barrack to eat and to be able to sleep to forget about the whole thing. They forced them all to stand there, and three companies of us [? has ?] [? suddenly ?] arrived and circled the whole parade ground with heavy machine guns, and then suddenly the reflectors came up, and they board three Jewish boys, my brother and his two friends. They were all very haggard. Already-- apparently, didn't feed them anything there. They stepped on the platform that they prepared for them, and everyone was looking, and the SS. And then suddenly, all three of them started shouting that every could here it, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. We are the last one. We are the last-- we are the [GERMAN]. Courage, in other words, they didn't worry about their death. They worried about us, that we should not be demoralized.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: That means [GERMAN], hold your head high. Don't despair. We-- they the last ones. Nobody else will be hanged anymore. Nobody has to be murdered anymore, because the victory is close, and the end is near. And-- and-- and it shook up even the SS. The SS didn't know what to say, because they were used that people cry and beg for their lives. There was a-- they were so shocked that the commandant-- at that time, the commandant was [? Schwartz, ?] was his name, a real murderer. He looked like a murderer this man. He started, [SPEAKING GERMAN], hang them-- screaming. He-- he got mad. And then they took one by one, and-- and-- and-- they didn't die fast enough. They were still pulling them. And everybody-- everybody was staring at this. And then when they came to my brother, and they put the noose around him, he was again shouting, long live liberty! Long live liberty! Anyway, all three were so courageous, and they really died like heroes. It had such a morale raising impact. They really knew if people can live like this, and die like this, there is hope. And let me tell you something, those three-- and then, after they were hanging there, they forced in single rows everybody to pass by to see this. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and-- and people didn't know what to do. They were what we call a [GERMAN]. They-- they-- they-- they didn't know what to say in a sense. What-- those three people, while they were working, SS-- camp mason, so to speak. They had also a-- a room where they had all their-- their instruments and their-- their-- their work things and so, where they worked [? this. ?] They every night-- every night they at least fed 10 to 15 people. They had access, because their work in the kitchen, the store. They're organized.

speaker: interviewer
text: Every knew about them. They were considered the-- the-- the-- how to say it-- the saints of the camp. They was so concerned with survival, they have hundreds of people they helped, hundreds. Every knew about it, and they had to-- you know, they had to die. You know? That's it. Now-- now came the-- the danger if they find out that Leo Diament had a brother. You see? They didn't know that at the time. And there was in the camp a-- a branch of the Gestapo. They called this the political [INAUDIBLE]-- the political department, so to speak. And they had all the registration, all the names, of everybody that was in Buna.

speaker: interviewee
text: My luck was that in the same barrack that I slept, there was also a Jewish prisoner that was the chief bureaucrat in the political [INAUDIBLE]. In other words, he did all the-- the-- the-- the filing, the-- the-- the ordering, the-- the typing, the-- the-- the arranging. And he arranged that my files somehow will be misplaced, so they will not know that there is a brother, because they would automatically have assumed that I knew about it, when in fact, I didn't know about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: We deliberated. I ask him, why didn't you tell me? And he told me, [? how ?] [? to ?] [? be, ?] my brother. If you would have known, you would have been with us together. Is that what you wanted? I wanted to save you. I did it for your sake.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now comes in-- in play again the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- the-- the-- SS [GERMAN] that was in charge of the kitchen. Came to Auschwitz, and he agreed to take me-- he was then sent to another camp to Gleiwtiz II, a small camp about 1,500 inmates. And he agreed that they transferred me to his camp, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because he knew about me, and for some, you know-- I don't know what motivated him. He was from the same town, so-- when they transferred me a week later to his camp, and I became his [INAUDIBLE]. In other words, I was his bed man, and cleaning room, and what have you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Freddy, there is no way for me to ask you how you felt about your brother's death--

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell it to you. I had guilt feelings that tortured me for at least two, three years, even after liberation. I felt almost guilty that I didn't do enough to prevent this. That I didn't-- that I should have known that he was involved in--

speaker: interviewer
text: What fantasy did you have about what you could have done?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I-- first of all, if I would have known I wouldn't have-- I would have told him, we lost our father already. We got, also-- while we were in Auschwitz, we knew there came a transport-- several transports from Riga, from the ghetto. So we knew that our mother is not alive, our two brothers are not alive. We even assumed that my sister is not alive, although she survived. She went to Stuttof, to another camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and-- and I would have told him, we were six children. If you and I survive, at least if it [? comes-- ?] [? come ?] [INAUDIBLE] there will be continuation of the family. Why didn't you tell me?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- I mean, it-- it-- I-- I was not allowed to talk to him, but I arranged it very carefully, and then always in-- in hiding so nobody should see me. He said, Freddy, you have to understand, I want to save you. That's why I didn't tell you. And I knew that if I tell you, you will be talk me out of this. He was an idealist. I told you, he was like this as a child already. He-- my brother was a true idealist. He believed in something, he was willing to give his life for it. And he felt, also-- he told me, everybody has to do as much as he can to save lives. That I have sworn to myself, he told me, and I-- I-- I did it, no matter what the consequences. He said, don't argue with me. But I felt so bad about it, because I still felt if I would have known about it, I would have talked him out of it. I could have been wrong, and-- and this guilt feeling for years I had this, that I had to lose my brother, you know, because we were five years together. And we [? lasted-- ?] it was October 10, 1944. It was exactly two months, before Auschwitz was evacuated.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you weren't in Auschwitz then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, it was Auschwitz III.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh--

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you-- you were not there when it got liberated. Where did you go--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in October?

speaker: interviewee
text: On-- on January-- on January 18, anybody that could walk was evacuated-- went on a death march from Auschwitz. And about 75,000 prisoners in the Auschwitz complex-- I told you they were 34 [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were in one of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, I was in-- I was-- at that time, I was in a small camp, yeah. I was at--

speaker: interviewer
text: Called Gleiwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, six weeks I was there. Our camp-- there were three main camps. It was Auschwitz, Birkenau, and Auschwitz III, Buna, where I was. That was-- we had 15,000 prisoners. And then when-- when-- when the order came-- interesting enough, when the order came from Berlin to evacuate Auschwitz, my lagerfuhrer, the former cooking-- kitchen chief-- listen to what he did-- took a truck and he said, let's go in Auschwitz the place where they stored all the individual private things of newcomers-- were stored called Canada, for some reason, because Canada was the symbol-- Canada is everything. Canada is [? gorgeous ?] to me. And this man went with a truck, and took me with the truck, and another have-- inmate to-- to go to Canada and whatever you can use to take back to Germany. It was so corrupt-- that's only with [INAUDIBLE]. Corrupt to the core.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he took the truck to Buna, to the camp where-- where I was before, because he knew the current commandant was a friend of his. And they were already all organized, standing, waiting to march out. And-- and-- I told him, can you leave me here? We-- we are going to be evacuated, and OK, stay here. So I stayed there with my former friends. There's another interesting story. I have to tell you what happened in-- in Buna, because it's very unusual. They sent to Buna in 1943 there about three groups of young people [HEBREW]. Do you know what [HEBREW] is? Preparing for aliyah to Israel. They were in farms, and they worked them-- they were on the farm, and they worked for the Germans, for the Nazis. And then in '42 and '43, they decided even them to deport to Auschwitz. And those groups came, there were three groups of 20, 30 kids, and they organized within the camp. They lived like in a kibbutz. They helped each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: And immediately when I found out to a [? lageralteste, ?] I made contact with him, and I helped him a lot. And-- and-- and we had sometimes even meetings. It was strictly forbidden. If the Nazis would have known that, that is-- if three people chat together and talked, that was considered conspiracy. And-- and-- the answer for conspiracy was death. That was the punishment. And-- and-- and we even had a Hanukkah party. I remember it. On-- in-- in-- in December of '43, we made the first time Hanukkah party in Auschwitz. And-- and-- and I remember [INAUDIBLE] is their leader. [INAUDIBLE] is their-- their-- of the group that was instructing them all the time, like they have a counselor in-- in a camps, you know-- in-- in-- in youth camps. I remember-- I would never forget this. He compared what we're going through with the situation in Israel when the Maccabees came into power, where the [INAUDIBLE] took over, the Romans took over, and everything was forbidden. And they forced them to-- to-- to [? prophetize ?] and they forced him to-- to-- to bring foreign gods into-- into-- into-- into a temple and what have you, and still, to survive, and they won in the end. And that will be with us too. We have to be strong. We have to never lose our optimism, never lose our will to survive, and eventually, liberation would come. And-- and then we would sing a Hebrew song. It was so dangerous, but we had guards outside. Even as [INAUDIBLE] guard forbids comes in, we got to-- immediately we have to disperse. I-- and-- and-- and we had-- there was a collaboration among those groups that is unheard of, because in Auschwitz, with all due respect, everybody was concerned primarily with his own survival, and rightly so. Because the first law was you survive, and then you can help others. If you don't survive, you can't help anybody, and nobody can help you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And here, those-- they work together to help each other. They-- they even trained each other. They-- they all claimed they are-- [INAUDIBLE] means they have to-- not a profession, no. What do you call it? Not a job-- they were either mechanics or electricians. What do you call this? They-- they were-- they had a-- not a professional. Trade.

speaker: interviewee
text: A trade. They're all-- that the best thing, if they were looking for tradespeople, I had a trade, because they needed them. Their chance of survival got twice as high if you had a trade.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, Freddy, tell me about liberation, or the-- the few--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --little bit proceeding the--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me tell you what happened. So on January 18th, they took 75,000 people, it took three days and three nights, to march back to Gleiwitz, because Gleiwitz had the biggest railway station in the whole area there. It was close to Katowice. It had a big railway station, and they were loading them-- for two-- for three days on cattle cars to ship them back into Germany. Listen to this. For some reason, we-- our group were at 15,000-- arrived the last at the railway station, and the-- and while we're arriving suddenly a Russian army patrol came so close. And they saw what was going on there-- [? need ?] to patrol. I would say, at most, they were two and a half kilometers. They started shooting with their cannon-- their cannons and their guns. It was an armored patrol. They had heavily armored cars, you know, not tanks, but-- but heavy armored cars. And the SS panicked. They were scared of it. They were afraid for their own lives, and they gave orders. [INAUDIBLE] were three or four big trains with-- with hundreds of cattle cars to stop loading and whoever is loaded go, because they were afraid that they will come. And those idiots-- if they would have done that, the SS would have fled. If the armored patrol had permission to-- to come close to the railway station, they could have saved tens of thousands of prisoners. But they didn't, either out of stupidity, or-- or because of orders they had not to make contact. And the rest were not loaded-- about 15,000. We have to march. That's the famous death march. This 15,000 column marched for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] eight weeks, or nine weeks, and it was the worst thing that I've ever seen in my life. It-- all the horrors that I've been through in Auschwitz in-- in the years and Sachsenhausen, is no comparison to what we suffered on the death march.

speaker: interviewer
text: The guards-- now listen to this. Guess who the guards were? You must have heard that the Grand Mufti of Jerusalem was invited by Hitler. The British wanted to arrest him, because he started the riots in '36 in-- in Palestine, and they reported him. And Hitler invited him, and he was sitting in Berlin throughout of war like a favorite guest in a palace. He had all the luxury. And in return for the favors of Hitler, he organized two Muslim brigades from Bosnia. Are you listening? From Bosnia. And-- and they were in the SS. They were all young men, I would say, from 18 to 24. They were the most barbaric, the most, I mean, blood thirsty, and-- and sadistic guards I'd ever seen.

speaker: interviewee
text: We marched. They didn't give us anything to eat, sometimes for two days, no drinks, no nothing. And it was one of the coldest winter in '44-- '45 that ever was in Poland. And the snow under-- we-- we marched on-- on the main streets, you know, the-- the-- the [INAUDIBLE]. And the snow was always shoveled to the side. And thirst is worst than famine and hunger. And if somebody stepped out of the column to grab a handful of snow, they right away shot him, and thousands were shot like this. Even if he was fast enough to go back and hide, they picked him out, and pulled him by his head and shot him right in front of it. The-- the-- the brain even was-- was-- was flying around. That-- that's what I said when you told me, for example, about what is happening to Muslims, how much they suffer in Bosnia from the Serbs, I said, it's inhuman, but who knows how many of those Bosnians are former SS guards. Who knows, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, that was my encounter with Muslim SS guards. We-- we marched about seven weeks, until we came to a small camp on the border between Poland and Czechoslovakia called Reichenbach-- another camp. This camp had 20,000 inmates, and they're-- they're all-- I think at that time, out of the 15,000, I doubt it if we were left 7,000, maybe.

speaker: interviewee
text: They put us there for two, three days, and pepped us up a little bit. Gave us a little bit of food, because sometimes we were sleeping on the ground in winter, in rain and snow. There was nowhere to put us. They were not prepared. I mean, they-- they had no-- no room for us. Sometimes for three days we didn't get any food. Then we got a potato, a stinking, rotten frozen potato. It was terrible. And people got so emaciated. I, myself, knew that if I go another week with this column, it will be the end. I was-- I-- I think I was reduced to, at best-- at best, my weight was 70 pounds, at best. I was skeleton like-- like you see in the pictures from Auschwitz. Now, let me tell you what I did. Because I was in-- in Buna, in Auschwitz III, I had connections-- you know, if you work for [? lageralteste. ?] At the end of Auschwitz, the last month, they didn't want to weave anymore the pajama suits. What they did is they took civilian clothes from the people that were arrested, and they took oil paint and made a big stripe that wide on the back of the jacket that was a sign that you are prisoner. Everybody in Germany, every policeman, knew anyone who wears a jacket like this is a prisoner.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, I want a jacket like this, and I got it. And on top of the jacket, I was wearing the striped pajama jacket. But every night, with my fingernails, I was scratching off the paint. And it was-- and over and over again, it-- it was not easy, but over and over again. And-- and-- I-- I was confident if I escape and-- and have a civilian jacket, my chances of survival are by far higher than when you go with pajama, because the Germans, when they saw somebody-- even the Poles-- with pajama, they knew it's-- it's an escapee from a concentration camp, and they report him immediately to SS. So we marched-- we're in Reichenbach two days, and then we marched out. And again, the same dilemma, we march several days, and I was totally emaciated. I just couldn't really anymore-- and I was on the border of losing my will to survive after so many years. Behind me was also-- of the group of the-- of the youngsters from the [INAUDIBLE], the [INAUDIBLE] groups. He was, for some reason, was stronger than me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, they-- the-- the number one guarantee for survival is good shoes, and they stole my shoes. I had first rate shoes. They stole my shoes at night when you sleep. Oh, yeah, everybody was out to survive. And I-- I had wooden shoes. That's the worst, you know, because before you know it you have all [?wounds?] all over the feet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I knew the chance for survival are nil. And-- and this guy behind me, that was matching behind me, often said, if you don't escape, give me the jacket. He constantly was nudging me-- nudging me. We arrived at a small town, [INAUDIBLE], near the Czech border, and while we were matching at night, on the main street, on both sides of the street were the-- the Germans. They're watching us, making fun of us, and nasty, nasty remarks, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. You deserve to die, and look what you've done to us. Now [INAUDIBLE] suffered, because of you-- all this garbage. While we were marching, of course, everything was light to the-- to the-- to the main street in the city. Suddenly, there was a Russian air attack. How do you know it was Russians? The Russians when they bombarded, they first throw-- they call it like Christmas trees, lights, so-- so to see the targets. And apparently, they had a full direct hit on the generator station, and for two minutes all lights went out. It was dark, and this guy behind me-- I always used to say, [LAUGHS] years later, it still hurts me-- gave me a [INAUDIBLE] in the tuchus and said, if you don't run, I take it away from you-- run. And for some reason, I said, he's right, and I ran.

speaker: interviewee
text: I ran all night. I-- I threw away my jacket, and put on the-- the-- the-- the civilian jacket, and scratched again, scratched again. I had some-- some old sea pants below my-- my-- my-- my striped pants. I-- I looked like a civilian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And let me tell you, I wasn't the only one. Over 100 people ran away, but the SS had dogs immediately ran after them, and-- and-- and I think maybe who-- guess who else escaped at that time that I found out 30 years later? [INAUDIBLE]. [LAUGHS]. They were-- [POINTS] they-- I didn't no about it. They wear also the same things, and they ran away, and they saved themselves too. But most of them got caught, and shot right in front of everybody to-- to-- to deter the others. So I ran all night, like an animal-- the [? instinct. ?] In the morning, I came to a major [INAUDIBLE], a major street-- a road. And it was full of [INAUDIBLE], like the one that went to California when California was discovered. This Volksdeutsche, this ethnic Germans, they're running away from the-- from the-- from the Russians. You know there were-- there were Volksdeutsche in Hungary, in Romania, in Poland, and they were the first target. When the Russians conquered a city, they shot them on the spot. They were considered traitors, and they ran away from them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I mixed among them, because that was it an advantage. I spoke German. And there was only chaos, let me put it this way. The SS was panicking, because the Russians were chasing them day and night. They were on their way to Berlin. And-- and-- and the Germans didn't ask me for papers, the-- the-- the ethnic Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: They gave me food, whatever they had. What I didn't know is that every kilometer was a checkpoint with SS men and-- and German soldiers, and they recruited anybody from the age of 16 to 60 in the army, because there weren't enough soldiers, and I got twice in order to present myself to be recruited. Now can you imagine? They see my number, and see I'm circumcised, that is death on the spot.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my luck was, while I was in Germany in a gymnasium, they study. Seven years Latin, four years French, three years English, and if you had a-- a-- a-- a-- how you call it-- a-- a career in the-- in the humanities, you have to learn Greek also. So anyway, I had a knack for French, for some reason, and many French Jews in '43 came to Auschwitz, they were [INAUDIBLE]. They knew Yiddish, but they spoke only French, so I'm in it with them and-- and just [INAUDIBLE]. I learned French and I spoke fluently French almost without an accent. I said, the only way I can save myself, I become a Frenchman.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you get among them, because there were in Germany at that time, one and a half million French slave laborers. I told you. They were semi prisoners-- semi-- but they had an F, and they had to work. And at night, they kept them in-- in camps. They weren't allowed to out at night. So I'm a Frenchman. I made up a name, a story. Where are your papers? What was your name? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Andre [INAUDIBLE]-- yeah, yeah. [INAUDIBLE] was the city where I come from. Where are your papers? I said-- and I-- I was wasn't [INAUDIBLE]. I was sick in Auschwitz. I had-- how you call this? [INAUDIBLE], all yellow. How you call this? I forgot-- When you get all yellow. What's the name of the sickness? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I can't think of it either.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you know what I'm talking about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. And I said, I was in a hospital, and there was suddenly an air attack, and I-- the whole hospital went up in flames, and I [INAUDIBLE] naked, jumped from the window, and some merciful Germans gave me some clothes. And-- and I spoke-- my French was better than that. Let's put it this way. I was interrogated by the Gestapo, and eventually believed me, and they took all the Germans, the ethnic Germans, and sent them to Czechoslovakia. They didn't want them to send them back to Germany not to frighten the Germans. To Czechoslovakia and the city [? Budjovice, ?] Budweiser. The Budweiser that-- [? Budjovice ?] is Budweiser. It-- it's a famous Czech town where they make the beer, and they adopted that. And I work for a Czech family. And the name, [? Benes ?] who was once the president of it, is like Smith. And my name is [? Benes, ?] and I-- they adopted me. I slept there, and I never told them that I escaped from a concentration camp, because I knew there was not a Czech family in all Czechoslovakia that at least one was killed, or in the concentration camps. And I would endangered their life, or they might get frightened, and report me to the-- to the Gestapo.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm the Frenchman. And, by the way, I spoke-- when I spoke with the Germans, I spoke half French, half German deliberately, so they couldn't understand me. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] half and half like-- like Frenchmen did. They spoke half German, half French. I learned this, because I had contact with those people. And I was there from the mid of March until the end of April. And they gave me [NON-ENGLISH]. It's-- it's [NON-ENGLISH] and-- and-- and-- Butter.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]-- no, [NON-ENGLISH] is fat-- fat. They-- they [? put ?] me up, because I was totally emaciated. I never took off my clothes. God forbid, they see my number. It was terrible. And I was afraid also, more than anything, that at night, I might talk in my sleep. I was afraid, or get frightened. I had nightmares then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's wait.
